text: 1. With the increasing of cold rolling reduction, the yield strength is significantly enhanced up to \~2.0 GPa and the elongation decreases. The strengthening mechanism of the present steel with low rolling reduction of 10–30% is the refinement of the slip bands spacing. At higher reductions of 50–90%, the Hall-Petch effect induced by microbands, twins, dislocation tangles and grain refinement plays an important role in enhancing strength.

2. The deformation substructure evolution during cold rolling approximately follows the sequence of Taylor lattice, microbands, twins, shear bands and dislocation tangles. Among them, the deformation twins are activated with the stress much lower than the critical stress, which may due to the short range order phase and the local stress concentration.

3. The intensity of the whole texture is relative weak, which may due to the planar dislocation glide. A “copper-type texture” is formed at 50% rolling reduction while it gradually transformed to “brass-type texture” with further rolling. The transition is attributed both to the dislocation planar glide and deformation twins. # Data availability The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study. # Declaration of Competing Interest The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. # Acknowledgements The authors acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 51871062 and 51421001).
